data.title: The Trade War Trapdoor
data.description:
Stocks crashed today for no apparent reason. Try as they might, analysts couldn’t pinpoint a catalyst, then stumbled upon a trade policy proclamation from the White House yesterday. That might have been it.

Level Change 4/3/25 (%)

-4.0 Dow

-6.0 Nasdaq

-5.4 Nasdaq 100

-4.8 S&P 500

-6.7 S&P 400

-7.1 S&P 600

Indeed, the worst crash since covid was all about President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement landing heavier than expected. Making the 1930 Smoot-Hawley effort look like a bake sale, he raised America’s tariff wall by sevenfold, to an average import duty of nearly 23%, up from a quaint 3%.

According to the World Trade Organization, here’s the new landscape, before retaliatory fun begins:

Average Tariff Rates (%)

23 USA

12 India

8.4 South Korea

6.7 Brazil

6.3 Thailand

6.1 Saudi Arabia

6.0 Chile

5.3 Russia

5.1 Vietnam

3.9 Mexico

3.4 Canada

3.3 UK

3.0 China

2.7 EU

1.9 Japan

The VIX volatility gauge took one look at that and vaulted like a biotech stock with a rumor and no revenue, surging 40% from 21.5 to 30.

Wall Street’s dunk tank was set extra deep. The S&P took its worst drenching since June 2020 when second-wave covid fears spiked sanitizer sales. The Dow notched its fourth-biggest decline this decade. The Nasdaq fell its most since March 2020, when the Fed cut interest rates to near zero, confirming there was something to be nervous about.

Small-cap stocks officially entered bear-market territory, with the S&P 600 now down 21.9% from its November 25 peak, the very month when now-Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent penned his Wall Street Journal valentine to “Trump’s Economics.”

In it, he credited the post-election rally to “expectations of higher growth, lower volatility and inflation, and a revitalized economy for all Americans.” From last Sunday’s Kelly Letter: “Nothing crashes harder than high hopes…”

Retaliation threats are stacking up faster than disclaimers in a crypto prospectus.

China vowed a stiff response unless the tariffs are lifted. Canada, keeping it neighborly, will match the 25% auto tariffs tit-for-tat with an equal levy on American vehicles. The EU is calibrating a response. France’s prime minister called the tariffs “a catastrophe for the economic world.” From a country that’s handled downturns with decapitation, that’s saying something.

Mexico, however, twirled into a hat dance after being spared the rod, one of the few free-trade partners to dodge it. Avocado toast is safe, and really, isn’t that what counts?

The Trump administration remains serenely convinced that the duties' dragnet will yield lasting economic bounty. Commerce Secretary Lutnick assured CNN viewers that Trump will not back down from this “reordering of global trade.” The president himself told reporters, “The markets are going to boom.”

Bears replied, “Oh, there was a boom, all right.” They doubled down on stagflation warnings, brushing off the idea that recent declines have priced in the damage. No, they say, 2025 earnings estimates show valuations have plenty of altitude left to lose. Beatings will continue until ratios improve.

Fedspeak played along. Vice Chair Philip Jefferson detected uncertainty in the air and craves more data. Fed Governor Lisa Cook foresees lower growth and higher inflation, citing “tariffs and other policy changes.”

Let the trade negotiations begin, before the damage requires a stimulus package and a cold compress.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ENOR: Trending Well, With Favorable Prospects
data.description:
Norwegian Equities Have Gotten Off To A Good Start This Year, But ENOR Isn’t The Only Way To Tap This Terrain

The iShares MSCI Norway ETF (BATS:ENOR), which covers around 59 stocks (mainly mid-caps which account for 43% of the portfolio) from the second largest economy of the Nordics region has gotten off to a solid start in 2025; during a period, where global stocks haven’t really gone anywhere, and European stocks have done well to witness gains of 11% (on average), ENOR has surged ahead by 15%.

Note that ENOR isn’t the only ETF product around that gives you access to this hotspot of Norwegian stocks; you also have the Global X MSCI Norway ETF (NORW), which is more popular (AUM of $50m as opposed to $32m for ENOR), and has been around for a longer period (it got listed in November 2010, while ENOR followed suit only in January 2012).

Even though these products are backed by competing fund houses, note that they both track the same index - the MSCI Norway IMI 25/50 Index. In light of this scenario, it would make sense to go with a product that is structurally superior. Unfortunately, it is difficult to pick a clear winner, as both products shine in different ways. NORW is the cheaper of the two, with an expense ratio of 0.5%, but ENOR is more rewarding from a yield angle (a higher current yield of 5.48% as well as a superior historical track record). Ultimately, it will boil down to each investor’s preference. Are you someone who attaches importance to the cost economics, or someone who wants a higher yield?

What Do The Norwegian Macros Look Like?

After a fairly resolute Q2 and Q3, where Norwegian GDP had grown by 5.3% and 3.7% YoY, the performance in Q4 was disappointing, with growth declining by -0.3%, as against expectations of just flattish growth.

However, this will likely be a blip, as real GDP growth for the whole of FY25 will expand by 1.8% (better than the 1% growth seen across the whole of 2024). What’s particularly enticing to note is the healthy conditions of the Norwegian consumer. After benefitting from wage growth of 5%, last year, Norwegians will likely see another hike of 4% this year, which will help boost purchasing power.

What abets their position even further is the broad slowdown in consumer prices, which has come off in a big way from the +6% levels seen in 2023 (admittedly, the latest reading for February 2025 was something of an unexpected surprise, growing at 3.6%, the highest in 10 months, but one swallow doesn’t make a summer and all that). Basically, with the onset of positive real wage growth, don’t be surprised to see the performance of household consumption contribute in a big way (expectations of 2.8% growth this year, as opposed to just 1.2% last year) to Norway’s growth prospects. Already in January, household spending rose by 6.4%, the best performance in 9 months.

Meanwhile, the forward-looking consumer confidence metric, while still in negative terms, has been improving over time, and saw its lowest drop in 12 months.

So far, so good, but what could give these households a real fillip could be further cuts in the policy rate. We say this because Norwegian households are one of the most indebted households in Europe with a debt-to-income ratio of over 200%, and personal savings too is quite low at single-digit percentage.

The central bank was actually on course to cut rates in last month’s meeting, but the latest inflation reading undoubtedly put them off. Note that this doesn’t necessarily mean that the prospect of further easing has been taken off the table. Rather, it's fair to say that it has only been postponed, as the Norges bank is still budgeting for its policy rate to drop to levels of 4% by the end of this year (from current levels of 4.5%).

What Do The Valuations Look Like?

Despite the strong price performance this year, the valuation backdrop of Norwegian equities still shines bright, relative to their Nordic counterparts. Note that on a P/E basis, Norway is the cheapest option around, priced at less than 11x, a +20% discount to the peer set average.

Given this low multiple, one would also expect ENOR’s holdings to facilitate the lowest weighted average earnings growth within the Nordic bunch, but that certainly isn’t the case here. In fact, other than Danish equities (which are anyway priced at a premium P/E of 15x), none of the other Nordic regions are currently offering a better earnings runway than ENOR (long-term earnings of almost 10%, which is 200bps better than the peer set average).

Closing Thoughts - What Do The Charts Suggest?

Even the charts ENOR point to healthy conditions.

Firstly, take note of ENOR’s short-term daily chart. After spending the whole of February, just below the landmark 200DMA, it finally broke through and is now comfortably trading above it. This technical development would likely have stimulated a lot of bulls to come on board. This was then followed by another technical event - the occurrence of the golden cross pattern, where the 50DMA overtook the 200DMA. At this juncture, ENOR is in a good place for momentum players, as it is perched above all its 3 key moving averages.

Despite the rise this year, ENOR could still work as a suitable mean-reversion bet for European-focused investors, as its current relative strength [RS] ratio versus a broader basket of European stocks is still around 13% lower than its long-term mean of around 0.42x.

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of Q2 2025
data.description:
The first quarter of 2025 was a tough one for the stock market in general for various reasons, but semiconductor stocks got hit harder than most as they look to make a fresh start in Q2 2025. While some may argue the selloff in semis presents a good buying opportunity going into Q2 2025 with many semis available at much lower levels, especially for those who missed out on the earlier rally, semis are unlikely to be in the clear just yet. Why will be covered next.

Semis underperform in an already difficult market

A previous article at the start of Q1 2025 mentioned how semis needed a lot to go right in 2025, which was unlikely to happen. Semis, for instance, are facing unique challenges that most other sectors do not. Semis have to contend with potential headwinds such as tariffs like other sectors, but they also have to deal with other challenges specific to the sector, like export restrictions to other countries and China in particular.

However, these limits are giving China an incentive to work on alternative sources of whatever is restricted, in effect giving rise to increased competition to many of the established players in the semiconductor industry. In the end, the article concluded the most likely outcome in the new year was for semis to underperform and end the year down.

A quarter has passed and it is fair to say that semis as a group have found it tough going in 2025. The chart above, for instance, shows how the iShares PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks 30 stocks active in the space, underperformed with a loss of 12.68% in Q1 2025. In comparison, the SPDR S&P500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ trust (QQQ) also had to contend with a difficult quarter like semis, but they suffered comparatively less with losses of 4.55% and 8.28%, respectively.

The quarter was a bad one for semis, but it is worth mentioning how they got off to a good start in 2025. SOXX, for instance, was up as much as 10.6% for the year on January 22, but it is no coincidence semis have lost a lot of ground since then. The Trump Administration that took office was expected to pursue policies that are problematic for stocks, but they were also expected to be offset by other policies that should favor stocks.

It’s still early, but it's been more of the former, including an emphasis on the use of tariffs, and not as much of the latter. However, while not the only factor around, it was the shaking of the foundation underpinning the artificial intelligence or AI story that Q1 2025 will probably be best remembered by. SOXX, for instance, suffered its biggest drop in Q1 on January 27 with very heavy volume, which can be attributed to the release of a new AI reasoning model from China’s DeepSeek.

China was previously singled out as the most likely source of curveballs for the semiconductor industry and one was certainly thrown with the release of DeepSeek-R1 in January. Semis have greatly benefited from AI, particularly when it comes to certain names, but the entry of DeepSeek into the equation has put some strongly-held beliefs into question, as will be seen later.

Who was hot and who was not

Semis are down as a group, but some did manage to squeeze out gains as can be seen with SOXX. The 30 companies present in SOXX are Texas Instruments (TXN), Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM), Intel (INTC), KLA Corp (KLAC), Lam Research (LRCX), Micron (MU), Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), Applied Materials (AMAT), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Analog Devices (ADI), ASML (ASML), Microchip (MCHP), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company or TSMC (TSM), Marvell (MRVL), ON Semiconductor/Onsemi (ON), Entegris (ENTG), Teradyne (TER), Skyworks (SWKS), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), Qorvo (QRVO), Universal Display Corp (OLED), Onto Innovation (ONTO), STMicroelectronics (STM), MKS Instruments (MKSI), ASE Technology (ASX), United Microelectronics Corporation (UMC) and Arm Holdings (ARM). INTC has struggled in recent years and it ended 2024 last among those present in SOXX with a loss of over 60%, but Q1 2025 turned out different with INTC coming out on top with a gain of 13.27%. INTC was able to achieve this because of how far it had fallen with a lot of negative sentiment, which flipped on its head after the optimism generated by the changing of the guard with new leadership, even if not much has fundamentally changed as of yet.

Speculation about a stock was also the reason behind the outperformance of runner-up UMC, which trailed only behind INTC with a gain of 10.17% in Q1 2025, except that the speculation revolved around a merger. Still, winners were the exceptions because only 6 out of the 30 stocks in SOXX ended with gains in Q1 2025. In contrast, 24 out of 30 head into Q2 2025 having suffered losses in the preceding Q1.

Of those who lost ground, MRVL was the worst off with a loss of 44.26%, followed by ON. The latter was adversely affected by problems, and a huge selloff in the stock, at an important customer, which happens to be the leader in the market for electrical vehicles in the U.S. However, the list of losers headed by MRVL is telling.

MRVL is one of those names whose bull thesis rests in large part on AI, together with names like NVDA, AVGO, TSM and others. All benefited greatly from their exposure to AI in past quarters, although MRVL has arguably not demonstrated as much benefit from AI as the other three, but this changed in Q1 2025 because all four underperformed the already underperforming SOXX. AVGO, NVDA and TSM still lead in terms of TTM gains, but the more recent struggles among AI plays clearly stands out in Q1 2025. DeepSeek left its mark in Q1 2025.

The table shows a few more things. Not only have semis lost the momentum they had until mid-2024, but they have gotten progressively weaker in the last three quarters. Most of the losses in Q1 2025 also came in the latter part, March in particular. SOXX, for instance, lost 9.76% in March and 12.68% in all of Q1 2025. On the other hand, the large losses suffered in the last month of Q1 2025 could enable a short-term bounce in Q2 2025, especially if semis can get some relief from the headwinds that caused problems in Q1 2025.

Are semis a buy in Q2 2025 after the decline in Q1 2025?

SOXX is close to 30% below the peak set in July 2024 and a number of semis, including some of the more highly sought-after semis, are down even more. Tariffs have weighed on stocks lately, but President Trump has shown flexibility on the issue. He delayed the initial tariffs on Mexico and Canada, for instance, and the stock market reacted well when he did.

Good news on the tariff front could trigger another relief rally, especially after the large drop many semis experienced last month with some like SOXX going almost vertical at one point. If the intent of the U.S. is for tariffs to be a temporary tool to extract concessions beneficial to the U.S. and the Trump Administration is seen to be working on other government policies that are more beneficial to stocks, like say tax cuts, then a rally is not out of the question for semis.

However, while a temporary bounce in semis cannot be ruled out, the March lows are unlikely to have been the bottom for semis as a whole. There is a reason why this could happen. The main goal behind the tariffs is supposedly to increase U.S. production of goods to lessen the need for imports. President Trump said as much when he remarked how companies can avoid the tariffs if they move production to the U.S.

Companies have the choice to move production, which cannot be done quickly, or pay the tariffs and decide whether to absorb the additional cost, or more likely pass it on to the consumer, but the end result either way will be an increase in prices for the end consumer. This will increase inflationary pressure, which is already an issue in the U.S. This could put the Federal Reserve in a dilemma.

The Fed has been easing monetary policy by cutting the Fed Funds Rate or FFR, which currently stands at 4.25-4.50%, down from a peak of 5.25-5.50% in July 2023, the last time the Fed hiked interest rates. The FFR was cut by 100 basis points in 2024 and, while there have yet to be any cuts in 2025, the most recent dot plots from March suggest two more rate cuts of 25 basis points each to bring the FFR to 3.75-4.00% by the end of 2025. In 2026, the FFR is seen to drop by a further 50 basis points to 3.25-3.50%.

In contrast, market expectations are even higher. Futures predict the Fed will be even more dovish by lowering the FFR by 75 basis points with an end point of 3.50-3.75% seen as having the highest probability in 2025. The issue here is that with all the recent government policies, especially those dealing with tariffs, the Fed may be in a lose-lose situation.

If the Fed cuts twice in 2025, let alone the three futures expect, the Fed risks pushing up inflation, in combination with the effect tariffs have on prices. If the Fed does not cut, the Fed can ease some of the inflationary pressure in the economy, but the economy will also slow down further due to the absence of the expected stimulus and the impact of tariffs on consumption. Both are undesirable options, but, given the Fed’s mandate when it comes to keeping prices stable, the Fed is most likely going with the latter. The Fed could be on hold for a while.

This suggests the market is headed for disappointment if or when the Fed decides not to cut three times as futures have priced in or twice as suggested by the dot plots. It can be argued that no single factor has contributed as much as the Fed has in enabling the bull market in stocks of the almost two decades since the financial crisis in 2008/2009. Conversely, if the Fed refrains from providing the stimulus expected, in this case interest rate cuts, stocks, particularly growth stocks like semis, are unlikely to handle it well.

Should semis expect even tougher trade restrictions from the U.S. government?

There is another reason why semis may not be in the clear. Tariffs have dominated the news cycle lately and they affect everyone in one way or the other, but there are other headwinds out there, including some that are specific to semis. Export or trade restrictions are another headwind for semis and there is reason to believe there will be more of them in the not so distant future.

First, it is important to mention that there is a strongly-held belief in the U.S. that whoever has the most compute will come out on top, whether it is in AI or some other strategic area out there. This belief is reflected in various actions taken within the U.S. For instance, U.S. export rules that seek to divide the world into haves and have nots by limiting the supply of AI chips to some countries, but not to others, have this belief as its core. So are things like Project Stargate, which seeks to invest $500B in new AI infrastructure in the U.S. There are many other examples.

If the U.S. has more compute, and everything related to compute like datacenter infrastructure and so on, than everyone else, then the U.S. has an advantage that proponents believe will ensure the U.S. stays on top. To ensure the U.S. has the edge in compute, it is necessary to limit what potential rivals can have, China in particular.

This means putting trade restrictions on anything that these potential competitors need to increase the amount of compute available for whatever that requires large amounts of it, like AI or whatever. This importance attached to compute is still present, including among decision makers, which suggests semis should expect more trade restrictions, especially whenever someone is seen to be gaining ground in compute, like in the case of China for example.

Is compute really as decisive as it is made out to be?

This race to have as much compute as possible has worked wonders for semis, particularly those engaged in high-performance computing. It is what caused, for example, a spike in demand for server GPUs from the likes of NVDA and CPUs from the likes of AMD, which were fabbed by a foundry like TSM and packaged by an OSAT like ASX using manufacturing equipment from the likes of ASML / AMAT / LRCX / KLAC and made from materials provided by the likes of ENTG, and so on. There is a spillover effect at work here.

It led to huge sums being invested in, for example, datacenters and everything else that goes in it, which in turn showed up in windfall sales at related suppliers. Some semis have expressed opposition to export rules limiting trade, but proponents of the policy have argued that companies will not be greatly affected because whatever is lost in terms of exports will be compensated elsewhere.

If, for instance, other countries do not have the compute necessary to service demand, then they will need to get it from somewhere with the means, most likely the U.S. So chip suppliers do not necessarily have to lose overall sales if they can no longer export because demand in the U.S. will be there to offset it. However, the hypothesis that whoever has the most compute is destined to win is debatable.

Compute is needed, but the amount needed to achieve a specific objective can vary greatly, depending on a whole range of factors. Someone may need more of it, but someone else could need much less of it to achieve comparable results. This can happen if, for instance, the latter uses a different algorithm than the former.

If A comes up with an improved algorithm with a time and space complexity of O(log(n)) and B continues to use an existing algorithm with a complexity of O(n^2) or O(n.n) to get the same results, then A will need less compute resources, negating the advantage B may have in terms of available compute. In other words, the argument that “whoever has the most compute will win”, which has been at the core of much of what has happened to the semiconductor industry in recent times, needs to be postfixed with “all else being equal”. If not, then it is not impossible to lose to someone else despite having the edge in compute.

Has DeepSeek changed anything for semis?

This is where DeepSeek enters the picture because it offers a reminder that it is not all about compute. While some may question the accuracy of some of DeepSeek’s claims, like in how much was spent on training, there can be no denying that the DeepSeek API is available at a fraction of the cost of what the other leading AI models go for.

This is consistent with DeepSeek requiring much less compute resources for training and inference because it relies on algorithms that are much more efficient in terms of resources needed than what other models use. If compute is important, but not all that is important, then that could affect how much resources, like capex, should be spent on it, and by extension the semiconductor chips needed for compute.

Semis have thus lost some of their luster due to DeepSeek. In a world where compute determines who wins, the importance of semiconductor chips cannot be stated enough. Paying very high multiples for selected semis can be justified since they are that important. But if compute may not necessarily determine the winner, semiconductor chips are still important, but not as much as before. Semis go down in priority accordingly.

It could also make investors think twice as to how high a multiple they ought to pay for a stock that may not be on the winning side in AI. The race to get to say AGI was until very recently thought to be between a few selected names, mostly the hyperscalers, but what the episode on DeepSeek suggests is that the breakthrough to get there might not come from the known players, but from someone most people may not even be aware of. There are no sure bets in AI since the race is still wide open, which argues against placing too high of a bet on anyone in particular because it comes at the risk of ending up with a losing bet.

If anyone wants to position themselves to possibly win the AI race, then doing so should involve more than just having the fastest chips and more of them than anyone else because this excessive focus on semiconductors, and the semis that make them, runs the risk of winding up on the losing side since other important areas get neglected. AI should not all be about compute, or semis for that matter. Not having the best chips is a disadvantage, but not something that cannot be overcome as long as there are other areas that can make up for it with good results, like analysis of algorithms.

Investor takeaways

The overall assessment at the start of 2025 was that semis would end the year down and nothing has happened with one quarter in the books to warrant a change in that assessment. Most semis posted losses in Q1 with SOXX losing almost 13%, but this comes on the heels of huge gains in prior years. There is room for semis to go lower.

The market still expects a helping hand from the Fed and the dot plots call for rate cuts this and next year, but this may not happen due to policy changes by the Trump Administration, which put the Fed in a tough spot in terms of monetary policy. If the Fed is forced to watch from the sidelines due to the risk of fueling inflation, and the economy slows down due to lower consumer spending as a result of higher prices, then growth stocks like semis could have further to drop.

The AI story underpinning the bull thesis for many of the most prominent semis is still there, but it suffered a blow from DeepSeek. The fact that many AI plays were among the worst performers in Q1 2025 says as much. The low cost of DeepSeek and other AI models out of China will make it even more challenging to generate returns on all the capital that has been invested. If the inflow of capex spending slows down because companies come to the conclusion there are little to no profits to be had, semis will lose a powerful tailwind.

The importance attached to compute has put semis in an enviable position, in both a good and in a not so good way. It has directed huge capital flows into the sector, but it has also caused governments to impose trade restrictions. This is not likely to change anytime soon. If anything, the U.S. government is likely to double down on the latter to slow down China’s progress in the field. China will respond by doubling down on ongoing efforts to come up with alternative options. DeepSeek is just part of that effort.

If Q1 2025 is to remembered in the history books, it could be in the sense it may have marked the beginning of end of the whole notion of compute as the ultimate decider of who wins in AI and other strategic areas of competition. There is a strong belief among a lot of people in the U.S. that having more compute than anyone else will be a decisive advantage, and a lot that has happened in the semiconductor industry can be traced back to it, but this faith in compute could be misplaced. Compute is important, but it may not actually prove to be the decisive difference.

Bottom line, if someone wants to put new money to work in 2025, then semis are probably not the best place for it. Individual names may do better, but the sector is more likely to underperform than outperform in 2025. Semis might make for a better investment opportunity down the road, but it is not likely to happen this year.
