data.title: Tariffs, Tension, And Turbulence: Global Markets React To Trump's Trade Shock
data.description:
By Andrew J. Willms

President Donald Trump's sweeping tariff announcement has reverberated through global markets, prompting a rapid reassessment of growth expectations, inflation risks, and investment positioning.

The policy introduces a 10% universal tariff on all imported goods, along with country-specific tariffs of up to 49% on select imports from nations including China, Mexico, and several European countries.

Far from a tactical move, these tariffs appear to be a structural element of the administration’s economic strategy - intended to shift global supply chains, rebalance trade flows, and revive U.S. manufacturing.

But markets, which had been bracing for clarity, instead absorbed a shock. Trading partners have already signaled intentions to respond in kind, heightening the risk of retaliatory actions that could dampen global growth and further disrupt supply chains

Market Response

Equity markets responded swiftly and negatively. The S&P 500 fell nearly 5% on the day, while the Nasdaq lost 6%, underscoring the market’s sensitivity to sudden shifts in trade policy. Tech, industrials, and consumer discretionary names led the decline. International equities also slipped as investors moved to reduce exposure to escalating geopolitical risk.

Losses were most pronounced in technology and consumer discretionary sectors, though selling pressure was widespread. International equities fell as well, though to a lesser extent, aided by more modest valuations and reduced exposure to U.S.-centric trade frictions.

Investor Considerations

The scale and abruptness of these tariffs have raised concerns about their potential to disrupt global supply chains, increase consumer prices, and dampen economic growth.

That said, although today’s moves were significant, they do not yet constitute a breakdown of long-term trends. For example, the S&P 500’s 50-day average is still above its 200-day counterpart, although the gap is closing and may soon turn negative if stocks fall further in the days ahead.

Moreover, U.S. stocks are now down more than 8% year to date (based on the S&P 500 Index), but bonds have rallied. The Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) is up 3.6% so far this year.

Diversification across asset classes has been a tough sell in recent years as U.S. equities - particularly tech - outperformed dramatically. But 2025 is once again proving the value of not putting all one’s investment eggs in a single basket. It is a principle that is a key component of The Milwaukee Company’s philosophy.

Conclusion

The new U.S. tariff regime has upended market expectations and introduced meaningful new variables into the global investment equation. While the immediate market reaction was sharp, it was not irrational - markets are doing their job by repricing risk.

For long-term investors, the challenge now is not to predict policy, but to adapt to it with clarity and discipline. That means respecting volatility without fearing it, and seeking resilience over reaction. If history is any guide, the eventual recovery may favor those who stay invested with purpose.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: CrowdStrike: AI Set To Supercharge The Value Of Its Platform
data.description:
Why I love Cybersecurity

While artificial intelligence will do many wonderful things for humanity, it will also equip hackers and adversarial governments with new tools to carry out there nefarious objectives.

In the 1990s and 2000s, bots, i.e., viruses, were spread by hackers, and they were developed such that antivirus software would not detect them.

Once a viruses infected a computing system, it would either operate in the background of that system indefinitely, or it would be eventually detected by the antivirus software, which would address the breach of security and eliminate the threat to the system.

These viruses were capable of many acts, such as stealing logins and passwords, using the information resources of the system for DDOS attacks, and stealing other sensitive information like credit card numbers.

Today, viruses perform the same acts; however, they've been supercharged via the advent of AI technology, which allows them to adapt within the computing system in real time.

Instead of a "dumb bot," or virus, which relied on its owners' commands, or a simple code set, today, these viruses can actively think while operating in the background of their infected host.

Instead of relying on the owner for navigating a computing system to achieve a desired outcome, such as theft of sensitive information, they can now actively reason themselves, discovering new pathways to achieve their nefarious objectives, 24/7/365.

With more and more sensitive information entering the cloud, i.e., being stored in computing systems, the stakes have never been higher, and the abilities of viruses have never been greater, and this trend is set to continue in earnest for decades to come. That is, viruses, aided by the latest AI, are set to evolve immensely in the decades ahead, creating huge room for competitive differentiation among the cybersecurity vendors in the marketplace today.

First, AI experimentation is just starting to evolve into AI outcomes. Second, we're in the midst of a rapidly accelerating geopolitical AI arms race. And third, winning the AI war requires the very best data and a battle tested innovation engine.

Commenting on the shift from AI experimentation to AI outcomes, we're still in the early, but rapidly evolving innings of the AI revolution. Businesses and governments across the globe are looking for their AI investments to yield both improved efficiencies and novel outputs. At CrowdStrike, we're requiring every team and function to leverage the power of AI.

George Kurtz, CEO, CrowdStrike Q4 '24 (CY) Earnings Call (emphasis added).

With this as our platform, let's consider one of the preeminent cybersecurity vendors, CrowdStrike.

Understanding CrowdStrike

CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWD) is a vertically integrated vendor of cybersecurity products, such as its identity protection product, cloud protection product, and its SIEM product.

CrowdStrike's Platform Graphically Depicted

We can see, in the above graphic, how CrowdStrike has layered various products and capabilities onto a single platform.

That is, CrowdStrike has built a vertically integrated, multi-product platform, and this is important to note for a variety of reasons.

Let's discuss.

As customers adopt more and more products, which is easy to do within a single, unified platform, they integrate more and more of their business operations and workflows into the CrowdStrike platform. This creates an embedding/switching costs moat, such that they are very unlikely to churn.

We can see evidence of this idea in CrowdStrike's 97% gross retention rate, which is a measure of how many customers stick with the platform on a year over year basis.

CrowdStrike's Gross Retention Rate In The Bottom Row

As we can see, CrowdStrike's gross retention rate stands at 97%, despite the very public and damaging IT outage that the company caused in mid-2024.

A strong embedding moat, as measured by a 97% gross retention rate, suggests very durable cash flows, which pulls up on a company's valuation such that the "risk premium" required by investors, i.e., the earnings yield above the risk free rate, such as the 10 year treasury bond's yield, is lower.

Strong embedding moat = more durable cash flows = a bigger valuation investors are willing to pay, because they know they can count on those cash flows through thick and thin, i.e., through the ebbs and flows of economic cycles.

In addition to creating a strong embedding moat for CrowdStrike, its vertically integrated, multi-product platform, on which it offers currently 29 different products, provides a host of other benefits.

For instance:

  1. More products = more avenues to go to market = faster growth and more efficient marketing spend, as CrowdStrike can show up to the right customer with the right product, increasing its odds of winning the business.
  2. More products = bigger total addressable market. If CrowdStrike offered just one product, its TAM would be limited to, perhaps, $5B. But, because it offers 29, that TAM is substantially larger. I will provide data illustrating this in a moment.
  3. More products = more valuable customers, i.e., with one product, a customer might be worth $100M to CrowdStrike, but, with 29, that same customer might be worth $5B over time. This is important to note because CrowdStrike currently has 74,000 total customers, which grew 30% year over year. And many of these customers have only adopted a handful of CrowdStrike's 29 total products. I provided data for CrowdStrike's customer product adoption below. Even if CrowdStrike never added another customer, it could likely grow to $10B in sales and beyond simply by upselling its existing 74,000 customers on its current product set. The reality, though, is that CrowdStrike will very likely both grow its customer base and expand its product set that it sells to those customers.
  4. More products = more durable cash flows. If CrowdStrike only offered one product, especially in the world of technology, there would be a risk of that one product becoming obsolete, imperiling its cash flow generation ability. However, because CrowdStrike has a diversified stream of revenues from 29 different products, there's far, far less risk of its cash flows being harmed by one or a few products falling into obsolescence as the technology landscape evolves.

CrowdStrike's TAM Expansion Via A Multi-Product Platform

The Growth Of Various Products On The Platform + Their Current Scale

Percent Of Customers With 5, 6, 7, And 8 Different Products

Our platform wins. Seven modules are each now individually over $300 million in ending ARR, demonstrating the power of our innovation in driving record adoption and Falcon's multi-[product] platform opportunity that continues to drive growth.

And our go-to-market execution momentum is unprecedented. Businesses are speaking with their wallets, investing in CrowdStrike to secure their futures. Today, including our end customers and those of our MSSPs, CrowdStrike is trusted by more than 74,000 organizations as their cybersecurity platform of choice, growing more than 30% year-over-year.

George Kurtz, CEO, CrowdStrike Q4'24 (CY) Earnings Call (emphasis added).

CrowdStrike's Net Retention Rate Illustrates Its Ability To Expand The Value Of Each Of Its Customers Over Time. (Top Row)

We want customers to engage with us with bigger deals, longer deals, that's exciting. Conversely, we now have a bigger base to sell into. We have this opportunity to go after more logos. So on any given quarter, we're going to get different dynamics and different pressures. But I think the good news is, we've got a lot of room with respect to new logos, and we've got this ever expanding base where we can sell into an upsell and cross-sell. So that's how we look at that dynamic.

Burt Podbere, CFO, CrowdStrike Q4'24 (CY) Earnings Call.

There are more benefits to being a vertically integrated multi-product platform; however, for the sake of brevity, I'll conclude here.

Let's now turn to a brief aside related to the CrowdStrike thesis, as well as all the next-gen software names I discuss with you.

Best Of Breed Tech To Benefit From DOGE

DOGE has, of course, been fashioned into a partisan issue; however, I would ask that you suspend your political predilections for a moment as I illustrate why the best of breed, next-gen software vendors are poised to benefit from the ongoing restructuring efforts of DOGE.

On CrowdStrike's last earnings call, as well as on the calls of a variety of software vendors, an analyst asked about DOGE's impact on their sales into the government market. This is what CrowdStrike's CEO, George Kurtz, had to say on the subject:

We find ourselves placed at the epicenter of a rapidly evolving demand environment. A new administration, a new wave of technology, and a new threat landscape necessitate all businesses to evolve their cybersecurity programs.

Consolidation, cost reduction and automation are now the accepted enterprise and federal priorities. These priorities are accelerating the shift from ineffective, narrow or duplicative point products.

George Kurtz, CEO, Crowdstrike Q4'24 (CY) Earnings Call (emphasis added).

I often share that software is a vast market that's not entirely constituted by the companies that are front and center in the public markets.

There's thousands and thousands of private company software vendors, who are often less efficient and less competent than their best of breed public market peers for reasons such as the public markets putting much more pressure on companies to perform to perfection, or at least as close as possible to perfection.

Pressure makes diamonds, so to speak, and the public markets can be something of a pressure cooker for companies: they must be excellent every. single. quarter, else their stock price, and by extension business narrative, gets thrashed.

As DOGE acts like a hurricane in the U.S. government, eliminating the ailing and inefficient aspects of the ecosystem, software vendors like CrowdStrike stand to benefit.

Let's now turn to a valuation exercise for CrowdStrike.

Valuation

Because CrowdStrike is one of the best executed software companies on earth, it's valuable to study its business to understand exactly what mechanisms have made it so great.

We've done this a lot already today; however, I'd like to work through these ideas via the four points of stock value, which will also serve to inform the assumptions we'll make for the valuation model below. Let's now walk through each of the four points of stock value together:

  1. Total amount of free cash flow per share, and margin understanding: CrowdStrike's free cash flow per share has consistently grown since it went public, and its margins have expanded as well. Following these four points, I'll share a graph of its free cash flow per share and margins, and I'll share the long term operating model the company shared with investors in recent years.
  2. The growth of free cash flow per share: Because CrowdStrike is a multi-product platform, who has demonstrated the ability to both organically develop new, growth-runway extending products and purchase growth-runway extending businesses, we can be sure that its growth runway remains extensive. Additionally, because cloud adoption remains in its early innings, and because malware is arguably the most dynamic and intractable issue facing businesses and governments around the world, we can be sure there's ample runway for growth ahead.
  3. The durability of free cash flow per share: As a mission-critical, vertically integrated, multi-product platform, CrowdStrike has a profound embedding moat, as evidenced by its 97% gross retention rate, which it's held despite causing one of the worst IT outages in history. This implies that CrowdStrike's cash flows are exceptionally durable.
  4. Our next best alternative's impact on CrowdStrike's valuation: The reality, notwithstanding the market's behavior today, is that interest rates are pretty low. The 2 year treasury bond, for instance, is below the long run American average interest rate of about 4%.

CrowdStrike's Long Run Target Operating Model (Note The Free Cash Flow Margin At The Very Bottom)

CrowdStrike Free Cash Flow Per Share & Free Cash Flow Margin

With all of this in mind, here are my assumptions for my valuation model:

Assumptions:

TTM Revenue [A]

 $4.2B

Potential Free Cash Flow Margin [B]

36%

Total diluted shares outstanding [C]

256M

Free cash flow per share [ D = (A * B) / C ]

$5.91

Free cash flow per share growth rate

22.5%

Terminal growth rate

3%

Years of elevated growth

10

Total years to stimulate

100

Discount Rate (Our "Next Best Alternative")

9.8%

Below, we can see the results of the modeling using the above assumptions:

Discounted Cash Flow Model + Assumptions Input + Dilution Considerations

Notably, I used a very aggressive 22.5% annualized growth rate; however, I do believe this is achievable considering CrowdStrike's multi-product platform, massive install base to whom it can sell more and more products, and TAM into which it's selling its various products, which itself should continue to grow as AI makes defending against malware threats more and more difficult.

Additionally, I did not dilute CrowdStrike's share count any further.

With ~$4.5B in cash and just $750M in debt, as well as robust free cash flow margins and free cash flow generation, CrowdStrike has ample resources to reduce the impact of stock based compensation over time.

In fact, as it matures in the years ahead, it's likely that it begins a share buy back program, leading to a reduction in share count.

Lastly, I used 36% free cash flow margins, i.e., the mid-point of CrowdStrike's long term guidance for free cash flow margins, and there's some chance they come in closer to 40% over time.

With these caveats in mind, let's now turn to total projected returns.

Total Projected Returns

As we can see, I used a 35x exit multiple for the 10 year modeling period, and I used this because CrowdStrike will very likely still be growing in the mid-teens by 2033, and its free cash flow per share growth could be closer to 20%, as a result of the virtually inevitable buy back that will come at some point.

With all of this in mind, we're being offered about 16% returns today, which I find somewhat underwhelming, but perhaps I have just been spoiled by the gains of the last few years from CrowdStrike.

That said, I do like CrowdStrike today within the context of a diversified basket of stocks; however, I don't love it. Hopefully, we get more selling from the Nasdaq such that CrowdStrike falls below $300/share, at which point we'd be getting much, much more torque from buying the stock.

But, again, down from $455/share and after a year of share price stagnation, I do like it here.

Summary

Following the CrowdStrike IT outage of mid-2024, CrowdStrike's growth rate was called into question; however, its Q1 '25 (CY) guidance has largely laid those fears to rest.

As can be seen in the chart below, CrowdStrike guided to 20-22% YOY growth for 2025, with net new ARR (annual recurring revenue) growth acceleration expected in the second half of 2025, implying accelerating growth through 2026.

CrowdStrike's Guidance For 2025

As for risks, at this point, they go without saying:

Cybersecurity entails protecting businesses in arguably the most complicated way known to man today.

And the level of complexity and the stakes for those protected have never been higher, and they are set to increase substantially in the decade ahead.

So breaches, lapses in security, and IT outages of various kinds are likely to occur again in the future, and this is a real risk for CrowdStrike, as well as all next-gen cybersecurity vendors.

I'll also add that CrowdStrike's valuation still implies perfectly linear 20%+ growth; so, should we enter a recession, which is always plausible, tariffs or not, CrowdStrike's valuation could decline by 50%, and I would consider that normal.

All that said, CrowdStrike, as well as the cybersecurity industry broadly, is set to grow for many, many years to come, and its share price should likewise grow over time.

data.title: Dell: Buy Now To Ride Its Growth And Margin Tailwinds
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Until now, I have not been bullish on Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) and that bias has avoided some downside, both on an absolute basis and relative to the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO):

Thesis

I am changing my stance on Dell and initiating a bullish view due to the following thesis points:

  1. Continued growth in the AI servers business and a PC refresh cycle creates bullish tailwinds
  2. Dell has a commanding position in the key growth segment
  3. A growth mix shift paves the way for margin expansion
  4. DELL stock has moved from a premium to a discount valuation vs. peers
  5. Relative technicals are at a key monthly support
  6. A worsening working capital profile is a risk to watch

Continued Growth In The AI Servers Business And A PC Refresh Cycle Creates Bullish Tailwinds

Dell's AI servers and networking business segment has been the key growth driver for the company over the past year, clocking in at least >30% YoY growth rates for the past 4 quarters:

Management is holding up bullish signs with their commentary as well:

Traditional servers, we saw 5 quarters of growth, and we have guided that we would have 4 more quarters of growth this year that we're in. We have the really the -- more opportunity with 14G transitions there. Storage, we saw growth again. We obviously talked about our strong performance in the last fiscal year, but expect that to continue into the year that we just started.

- CFO Yvonne McGill in the Morgan Stanley Media & Telecom Conference

The 14G transitions refer to the 14th technology generation of Dell's PowerEdge servers that are specifically designed to handle AI/ML and edge computing workloads.

There is a large untapped growth potential with the enterprise customer market, with whom Dell already has existing relationships via its PC business:

we talk about being in early innings with the Tier 2 CSPs [cloud service providers]. When we talk about the enterprise, we either say, hey, we're in very early innings or maybe we're still on our way to the stadium there, to the field, because we haven't really seen that take off very much yet. We have over 2,000 enterprise customers that are at the beginning stages of tests and -- within their companies, but not at scale… So I think of the demand environment. I think it's very robust. It's expanding. It's expanding both in the Tier 2 hyperscaler level, but it's also, more excitingly or equally as exciting, in the enterprise, right, where I do feel that we have even more differentiation. Our solution is differentiated and advantaged. When you get into the broader enterprise, you get to take advantage of all -- the rest of Dell's capabilities, right, and our scale, our global scale, and services and solutions.

- CFO Yvonne McGill in the Morgan Stanley Media & Telecom Conference

As for the PC business, a refresh cycle is a positive tailwind expected to kick in in the latter half of this year:

I'm so excited about the PC refresh. We are expecting that to take off as the year progresses, more weighted towards the second half.

- CFO Yvonne McGill in the Morgan Stanley Media & Telecom Conference

Overall, it looks like Dell is facing growth shoots in all its business segments.

Dell Has A Commanding Position In The Key Growth Segment

Dell has been facing market share pressures in its PC business for multiple quarters now:

But in the servers business, it has been steadily gaining share, and it has a dominating market position in storage solutions:

RAID stands for Redundant Array of Independent Disks. It is a method of storing data in multiple places (hard drives or solid-state drives) to have backups in case of drive failure.

On balance, Dell's share gains in growth-leading servers and storage business matters more than the PC market share declines, since Dell's overall revenue mix is increasingly being weighted toward the former:

This revenue mix shift has been occurring since Dell first shipped the XE9680 - the first 8-way GPU rack server designed for AI/ML workloads:

AI server pipeline continued to expand sequentially and has grown every quarter since the introduction of the XE9680

- Dell's Q4 FY 2025 Earnings Presentation

A Growth Mix Shift Paves The Way For Margin Expansion

Dell's fast-growing AI servers and networking business falls within its Infrastructure Solutions Group [ISG]. The EBIT margin profile of the ISG business is not only much higher than the Client Solutions Group [CSG] (which represents the PC business), but also increasing:

Therefore, I expect the combination of a revenue mix shift toward the higher and increasing margin business to lift overall company margins, continuing the trend of positive EBIT margin surprises:

DELL Stock Has Moved From A Premium To Discount Valuation Vs. Peers

The last time I covered Dell, the stock was trading at a 15.5% premium to its peers. But now, at a 1-yr fwd PE of 9.7x vs. the peer group median's 10.8x, DELL is now at a discount. I think the combination of favorable growth and margin tailwinds and a cheaper valuation makes the buy case more attractive.

Relative Technicals Are At A Key Monthly Support

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of DELL vs. SPX500

Relative to the S&P500, DELL is resting at a major monthly support area. The approach down has not been smooth, as evidenced by the presence of candle wicks. To me, this indicates slowing seller momentum, which I believe increases the chances of a rollover toward the bullish side.

A Worsening Working Capital Profile Is A Risk To Watch

If you're a regular reader of my analysis, you've heard this before, so excuse me for the repetition:

I think many investors focus disproportionately on accounting earnings metrics without much of a glance on how that actually translates to what ultimately matters; cash flow. So I often look at net working capital (and where relevant, capex) numbers to identify notable movements on those metrics too. And for Dell, I see that the net working capital days intensity has been worsening:

This means that whilst Dell was converting cash 82 days in advance before, now it is only converting cash 51 days in advance. Digging further into the drivers of this, I spot that the payable days have dipped from 88 to 79 days QoQ:

This means Dell has been paying cash to its vendors 9 days quicker in the Feb 2025 quarter, which would be a contributing factor to the sharp sequential fall in OCF margins (operating cash flow over revenues) in Q4 FY 2025 from 6.4% to 2.4%:

Now, a closer look at the payables chart above shows that the Feb quarter tends to see a sequential dip and then recovery in the May quarter. So, this may not be a major area of concern. However, given the broader declining trend in OCF margins, I still identify overall net working capital trends as a risk factor to keep on watch.

Takeaway & Positioning

I am becoming bullish on Dell due to strong, broad-based growth tailwinds in all major business segments. Next-generation product cycles and untapped adoption potential by enterprises is a powerful growth driver for the AI servers and storage systems businesses, both of which have a strong market share position. And the slower-growing legacy PC business is also poised to benefit a bit from PC refresh cycles later this year. I expect a continued revenue mix shift toward the higher-margin infrastructure solutions group to lead to margin expansion at the company level.

DELL stock has also moved into discount valuation territory since my last coverage, where it had traded at a premium. This, coupled with a bullish technical outlook, makes the buys more attractive.

One thing I noticed is a falling operating cash flow margin, indicating worse cash flow conversion. I am watching for unfavorable net working capital intensity trends to monitor this risk.

Rating: 'Buy'

How To Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: PTA: Suffering From Rising Rates, But A Decent Preferred Fund
data.description:
The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund (NYSE:PTA) is a closed-end fund that endeavors to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. This is a need that many investors have today, and it's particularly common among retirees. After all, for most of the past 15 years, it has been exceptionally hard to earn an acceptable level of income without taking on an excessive amount of risk. As we can see here, prior to 2022, the only time in the past 10 years that the yield of the 10-year U.S. Treasury note exceeded 3% was a very brief period of time in late 2018:

A 3% yield from a bond only results in $30,000 per year of income, which is certainly not sufficient to satisfy anyone who has managed to amass a $1 million portfolio over their working years. Today, yields are a little better, but even today’s 4.03% 10-year U.S. Treasury yield only results in $40,300 in annual income. Once again, that's too low to support anyone who has managed to acquire a $1 million portfolio over their lifetime. Other assets that may have been considered good ways for retirees to obtain income in years past, such as utility stocks and bank certificates of deposit, are likewise paying yields that are far too low to support a reasonable lifestyle in retirement. As such, better solutions are needed, and the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund might be one potential option for anyone in need of income.

As might be expected from the above paragraph, the current yield of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is much higher than many other things in the market. As of the time of writing, shares of this fund yield 8.14%, which is much higher than any of the major fixed-income indices. We can see that in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.75%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.68%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

6.55%

For the most part, these indices have higher yields today than were widely available prior to 2023. However, none of them are particularly impressive for investors who are in need of income. One major reason for this is that these yields are not that high in real terms. This chart shows the month-over-month change in the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index, which many economists (including those at the Federal Reserve) believe provides a better measure of inflation than does the consumer price index:

For February 2025, the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index rose by 0.4%, which was the highest level since January 2024. We can also see that this figure has been steadily increasing since November, which clearly suggests that inflation is accelerating. After all, the month-over-month number does a much better job of providing a real-time measurement of inflation than does the more widely reported year-over-year figure. As such, this is clearly pointing to inflation getting worse, which would be consistent with recent trends in alternative measures of inflation.

More importantly, however, a 0.4% month-over-month increase works out to 4.8% annually if inflation were to continue at the same rate for the next 11 months. If we assume this as the inflation rate, this reduces the real yields of the above three fixed-income indices to these levels:

Index/ETF

Real Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

-1.05%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index

1.88%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index

1.75%

Obviously, these are not particularly attractive real yields in the grand scheme of things. Indeed, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index has a very unattractive real yield as it's not even high enough for an investor to maintain their purchasing power. As such, higher yields than these are really needed in today’s environment, and the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund provides a way to obtain these higher yields. With that said, though, a 4.8% inflation rate does reduce the yield of this fund down to 3.34%. While that's not as attractive as some, if not many, investors would want, it's still a lot more attractive than what the fixed-income indices provide.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has historically had a lower yield than most of its peers, despite being fairly attractive relative to the major indices. Here is how it compares today:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

8.14%

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund (FPF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

9.04%

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund (FFC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

6.90%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

8.89%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Preferreds

10.06%

As we can see here, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund only has a yield advantage over the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund. In fact, the fund has a yield advantage over all preferred stock funds managed by Flaherty & Crumrine, but most funds from other fund houses boast higher yields. This might be a turn-off to some investors, particularly those who are looking to maximize the income that they receive from their assets. However, there could be some reasons to choose the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund, especially for risk-averse investors. For one thing, as we will see in this article, this fund historically has a lower level of leverage than most of its peers, so it should be somewhat less volatile over the long term. In addition, the fund’s current yield appears to be a vote of confidence from the market. As I stated in a recent article:

The market tends to value closed-end funds based on their yields. As such, when we see a fund with a yield that is substantially above that of its peers, it can be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its payout.

This works in reverse as well, as a fund with a low yield when compared to its peers is generally viewed as having a sustainable distribution by the market.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund obviously does not have an especially high yield relative to its peers. Indeed, it's lower than most of them so this appears to be reflective of a belief by the market that this fund will be able to sustain its distribution going forward. Naturally, we will need to take a closer look at this fund and make our own determination about that, but it's still a good sign overall.

Recent Performance Analysis

As regular readers can likely remember, we previously discussed the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund in early autumn 2024. My previous article on this fund was published to Seeking Alpha on Sept. 25, 2024. Thus, just over six months have passed since our last discussion. As we all know, a lot can happen in six months, and the most recent six-month period was certainly eventful. However, many of the events that occurred will not have a huge impact on preferred stocks because of the way that these securities work. For the most part, preferred stocks have no inherent link to the growth and prosperity of the issuing company. As such, things such as recessions, economic turmoil, and the most recent concerns about trade wars and tariffs do not really have a major effect on preferred stocks. At least, these events do not have as significant of an impact on preferred stocks as they have on common stocks.

One event that does have an impact on preferred stocks, however, is a change in interest rates or interest rate expectations. This occurred during the six-month period in question, due mostly to the resurgence of inflation that we discussed earlier in this article. Back on Sept. 25, 2024, the Federal Reserve had just cut its benchmark federal funds rate by 50 basis points, which proved to be the first of a series of cuts that reduced the federal funds rate by 100 basis points between September and December 2024. This was consistent with guidance that the Federal Reserve had previously provided that stated that it would start reducing interest rates once inflation was “sustainably on a course to 2%.” As we can see here, the headline inflation rate, as measured by the Consumer Price Index, had declined from 3.5% in March 2024 to 2.5% in August 2024:

This apparently led the Federal Reserve to believe that inflation was indeed on a path back down to 2%, so it started to reduce rates per its guidance. However, an unexpected (by most analysts) event began to occur once the central bank started to reduce its interest rate. Basically, the market started to fear that the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cuts were a mistake that would cause a resurgence of inflation. As the chart above shows, the market was correct, and inflation did start to increase immediately following the Federal Reserve’s first interest rate cut. As a general rule, rising inflation causes bond prices to fall and yields to rise as bond investors want to ensure that they receive positive real yields from their holdings. Thus, despite the expectations from many analysts that the central bank’s interest rate cuts would be a positive for bond prices, bonds fell over the period. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index fell by 4.24% over the period that lasted from the Sept. 25, 2024, publication date of my previous article through the end of the year:

This is important for understanding the performance of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund despite the fact that the fund invests in preferred stock as opposed to bonds. This is because preferred stock prices tend to exhibit a very strong positive correlation to bond prices. This was certainly the case over the period in question, as the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index fell by 5.98% over the September 25, 2024 through end-of-year period:

In short, the most important thing for preferred stock pricing is interest rates due to the fact that these securities are much more like bonds than like common stock. The dividend is typically fixed and has no relationship to the performance of the issuing company. As such, the prices of these securities vary so as to give any particular security a comparable yield to newly issued preferred stocks with the same characteristics.

With that said, preferred stock prices have not performed nearly as well year-to-date as bonds. The ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index is down 4.42% year-to-date despite domestic investment-grade bonds actually being up 2.01%:

This would seem to contradict my earlier statement that the price of preferred stocks tends to correlate with bonds, but it starts to make a bit more sense when we consider that domestic junk bonds have also fallen slightly year-to-date:

We appear to be seeing a “flight-to-safety” trade going on in the fixed-income market right now. This is, quite simply, when investors sell assets that they perceive as risky in an attempt to get into assets that they perceive as safe. Junk bonds and preferred stocks are both theoretically riskier than investment-grade bonds, so investors seeking a safe haven from the recent market uncertainty and turmoil might opt to sell their junk bond and preferred stock assets and purchase investment-grade bonds. I explained this in more detail in other recent articles, such as this one. Basically, companies that issue junk bonds tend to have fairly strained cash flows, so if some economic event adversely affects their cash flows, then it could cause them to default. The same may be true for preferred stocks, but most preferred stocks are issued by banks that will likely be able to weather an economic crisis. It is possible, though, that the current economic environment, which might actually result in stagflation, could be prompting a sell-off in fixed-income securities in general as investors demand higher yields to ensure continued positive real yields in the face of inflation. Investment-grade bonds have just not been negatively affected by this sell-off because of their perceived safe haven status.

As the above charts show, preferred stocks as an asset class have been broadly down since the last time that we discussed the fund. As such, we can likely expect the share price of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund to also have declined over the period. This is indeed the case, as shares of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund have fallen by 5.52% since our last discussion:

As clearly shown, the fund underperformed the investment-grade bond index over the period, although that was not really surprising. Investment-grade bonds, after all, have generally benefited from the flight-to-safety trade year-to-date, and they outperformed preferred stocks since the date that my previous article on this fund was published. The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund primarily invests in preferred stocks, so we would expect its performance to be more like preferred stocks than investment-grade bonds. Interestingly, however, we can see that this fund’s share price actually outperformed the domestic preferred stock index. This is somewhat more surprising since we would expect this fund’s leverage to result in its portfolio suffering larger declines than the index. We should have a close look at the fund’s valuation, as this performance might have resulted in the fund’s share price becoming overvalued.

Overall, the fact that the shares of this fund fell by 5.52% since our last discussion could turn off potential investors. After all, nobody wants to lose money on any asset. However, it's important to note that investors in this fund actually did better than the above chart suggests. As I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A simple look at the fund’s price performance could be misleading. This is because closed-end funds such as the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund typically pay out most or all of their investment profits in the form of distributions to the shareholders. The basic business model is to keep the portfolio’s size relatively stable while paying out all of the profits earned by the portfolio. As these funds do not retain their investment profits, its share price will not appreciate as much as an index fund that retains its unrealized gains (and rarely realized such gains).

However, the distribution itself does serve as a real return that benefits investors in the fund. As such, shareholders in most closed-end funds typically do much better than the share price performance alone would suggest. This also means that we should include the distributions in any analysis of the fund’s performance.

When we include the distributions that were paid out by the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund, as well as by both fixed-income index funds, we get this alternative chart:

Once again, we can see that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund outperformed the preferred stock index. In this case, however, it was by a fairly significant margin due to the fund’s higher yield. The fact that the fund pays more money every month to its investors than does the index means that its distributions were more effective at offsetting the share price decline than was the case for the index. However, the outsized distribution of the fund was still not enough to close the gap between it and investment-grade bonds.

With that said, the fact that investors in this fund are only down 1.66% over the six-month period is not really that bad given the overall turbulence in the market, and especially in the fixed-income market over this period. Overall, it is reasonable to be satisfied with this.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. This objective makes a great deal of sense given the fund’s strategy. This strategy is described in great detail in the fund’s most recent annual report:

The Fund pursues its investment objectives primarily by investing in issues of preferred and other income securities the investment manager believes to be undervalued relative to credit quality and other investment characteristics. In making this determination, the investment manager evaluates the fundamental characteristics of an issuer, including, among other characteristics, an issuer’s creditworthiness, and also takes into account prevailing market factors. In analyzing credit quality, the investment manager considers not only fundamental analysis but also an issuer’s corporate and capital structure and the placement of the preferred or income securities within that structure. The investment manager may rely primarily on its own analysis of the credit quality and risks associated with individual securities considered for the Fund, rather than relying exclusively on nationally recognized statistical rating organizations or third-party research. This aspect of the investment manager’s capabilities will be particularly important to the extent that the Fund invests in below investment grade or unrated securities and in securities of non-U.S. issuers. In evaluating relative value, the investment manager also takes into account call, conversion and other structural security features, in addition to such factors as the likely directions of credit ratings and relative value versus other income security classes.

…

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its Managed Assets in a portfolio of preferred and other income securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. companies, which may be either exchange-traded or available over-the-counter. Such securities may include traditional preferred securities; hybrid-preferred securities that have investment and economic characteristics of both preferred stock and debt securities; floating-rate and fixed-to-floating-rate preferred securities; fixed-and floating-rate corporate debt securities; convertible securities; contingent capital securities; and securities of other closed-end, open-end or exchange-traded funds that invest primarily in preferred and/or debt securities. These securities may be across a wide range of sectors, industries, and countries. To the extent the Fund invests in securities of other closed-end, open-end, or ETFs, the Fund will consider the investments of these funds, to the extent known by the Fund, in determining compliance with this policy. The Fund may also invest in restricted securities, including securities that are issued in private placement, securities that are only eligible for resale pursuant to Rule 144A under the Securities Act of 1933 and securities of U.S. and non-U.S. issuers that are issued without registration with the Securities and Exchange Commission pursuant to Regulation S under the Securities Act. In addition to purchasing securities in the secondary market, the Fund may seek investment opportunities in new issues and follow-on or secondary offerings of preferred and debt securities. “Managed Assets” are the Fund’s net assets, plus the principal amount of loans from financial institutions or debt securities issued by the Fund, the liquidation preference of preferred shares issued by the Fund, if any, and the proceeds of any reverse repurchase agreements entered into by the Fund.

The Fund will invest 25% or more of its total assets in the financials sector, which is comprised of the banking, diversified financials, real estate and insurance industries. From time to time, the Fund may have 25% or more of its total assets invested in any one or more of these industries that make up the financials sector. In addition, the Fund also may focus its investments in other sectors or industries, such as (but not limited to) energy, industrials, utilities, pipelines, heath care and telecommunications. The investment manager retains broad discretion to allocate the Fund’s investments across various sectors and industries. The Fund may invest without limit in securities on non-U.S. companies, which may be non-U.S. dollar denominated, including up to 15% of Managed Assets in securities issued by companies domiciled in emerging market countries. Typically, emerging markets are in countries that are in the process of industrialization, with lower gross national products per capita than more developed countries. Many foreign companies issue both foreign currency and U.S. dollar-denominated preferred and debt securities. Those securities that are traded in the United States have characteristics that are similar to traditional and hybrid preferred securities. The Fund may also invest in securities of foreign companies in the form of American Depositary Receipts, Global Depositary Receipts and European Depositary Receipts. The investment manager may hedge some or all of the Fund’s foreign currency exposure.

This description makes it quite clear that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund invests its assets primarily in preferred stock. After all, the second paragraph in this description explicitly states that at least 80% of the fund’s assets will be invested in preferred stock or other income-producing securities at all times. This normally means preferred stock, which is a hybrid security that sits between debt and common equity in a company’s capital stack. However, the description does not specifically exclude bonds as an asset type that the fund could potentially hold. In fact, it states that the fund considers both fixed-rate and floating-rate bonds to be eligible securities to meet this 80% mandate. As I pointed out in a previous article, there are some preferred stock funds that actually have a substantial proportion of their assets invested in bonds. As of Aug. 30, 2024, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund was one such fund, as it had 45.89% of its assets invested in corporate bonds, convertible bonds, and U.S. Government Agency securities as of that date. In fact, that particular fund still has a very high proportion of its assets invested in debt securities as opposed to preferred stock:

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has historically taken a different approach. This fund has been almost entirely invested in preferred stock every time that we have discussed it in the past. It appears that this is still the case today. As of the time of writing, the most recent schedule of investments that's available for the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is found in the most recent annual report and dated Oct. 31, 2024. Here's the fund’s asset allocation as of that date:

Asset Type

This is at least somewhat similar to the asset allocation that the fund had the last time that we discussed it. There have been a few changes, however, particularly where cash equivalents are concerned. Please recall that the last time that we looked at this fund, it only had 0.2% of its net assets invested in money market funds. That figure is much higher today, which almost certainly means that the fund sold off some of its securities and went to cash rather than deploying the capital into new securities. This conclusion is reinforced by the fact that the fund’s exposure to over-the-counter securities declined by quite a lot over the period. The full bevy of changes is briefly listed here:

The fund apparently sold off some of the over-the-counter preferred stock holdings that it previously owned. It appears that it likely deployed some of the money that it received from this sale into exchange-traded preferred securities. The remainder of the received cash was simply put into a money market fund to collect interest. The fund likely sacrificed some of its income making this trade, as money market funds have lower yields than most preferred stock. This is certainly the case with the money market funds that the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is using to manage its cash equivalents. Here are the two money market funds listed among the fund’s largest holdings:

As we can see, the two money market funds that are used by the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund are the State Street Institutional Treasury Plus Money Market Fund (TPIXX) and the State Street Institutional U.S. Government Money Market Fund (GVMXX). Here are the current yields of these two funds:

Fund

Yield

State Street Institutional Treasury Plus Money Market Fund, Premier Class

4.27%

State Street Institutional U.S. Government Money Market Fund, Premier Class

4.28%

On Oct. 31, 2024, which is the date on which the fund was holding 5.3% of its net assets in these two money market funds, the federal funds target rate was fifty basis points higher than it is today, so the yields on the money market funds were a bit higher. The annual report states that each of them had a 4.82% yield on that date, but money market fund yields tend to vary slightly from day to day, so we can assume that it was earning a yield that was within a few basis points of that. This is still substantially lower than what preferred stocks were yielding on the same date. Preferred stock yields have generally been between 6% and 7% for most of the past two years, so by storing its cash in money market funds instead of redeploying it into preferred stock, the fund apparently sacrificed some income. However, the domestic preferred stock index has been falling fairly steadily since the Oct. 31, 2024, reporting date:

Depending on what the fund did with this cash after the closing date of the annual report, it may have avoided taking losses by buying securities that were falling in price. If the fund is still holding its cash (and it has not yet released documentation stating whether or not that is the case), it has prevented itself from taking 6.82% of unrealized losses. If the fund did indeed manage to avoid those losses, this would be a shrewd decision on the part of its management that shows excellent market timing. It is, however, very rare for any fund to hold 5% or more of its net assets in cash for any sort of extended period, so it seems likely that it did deploy the money at some point between the closing date of its annual report and today.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield that it earns from the assets in its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase preferred stock, bonds, and income-producing assets. As long as the preferred assets have a higher yield than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, that will usually be the case.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not using too much leverage since that would expose us to too much risk. I do not generally like to see a fund’s leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 34.30% of its portfolio. The fund’s website states that the fund had a leverage ratio of 34.19% as of Feb. 28, 2025, so it seems likely that the fund’s current leverage ratio is pretty close to the figure stated by CEF Data given that preferred stock prices and the fund’s net asset value declined in March:

After all, as I have pointed out in various previous articles, a decrease in a fund’s net asset value will usually result in its leverage increasing assuming that the actual amount of debt that it has remains stable.

Interestingly, however, the provided leverage figures are slightly below the 34.60% ratio that they were at the last time that we discussed this fund. That's very surprising considering that the fund’s net asset value has decreased by 4.11% over the period:

The only way that the fund’s leverage ratio would decrease in the face of a net asset value decline is if it's actively reducing its debt in order to keep the leverage at a specified level. That is certainly possible, but it is rare for a closed-end fund to do this.

In the previous article, we saw that this fund generally uses less leverage than its peers. Here is how it compares today:

Fund Name

Leverage Ratio

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund

34.30%

34.91%

37.80%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

37.90%

(all figures from CEF Data)

As we can see, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has a lower level of leverage than does its peers. However, the fund’s leverage ratio is not vastly lower than all of them as the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund is only 60 basis points higher. Nevertheless, this fund does remain the least-leveraged of its peer group, and that will likely be attractive to those investors who dislike too much risk. It could be a real advantage today given the substantial volatility in the fixed-income market.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.1340 per share ($1.608 per share annually). This gives the fund a distribution yield of 8.14%, which is fairly attractive compared to the indices but is lower than most of the fund’s peers.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has a history of being quite consistent with respect to its distribution. We can see that here:

As I stated in the previous article on this fund:

In stark contrast to many other fixed-income funds, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has not had to decrease its distribution over the past few years. This is surprising, since the rising interest rates in 2022 would have almost certainly caused any securities that this fund purchased during 2020 or 2021 to decline substantially in price. As this fund’s inception date was in October 2020, that would almost certainly mean that it incurred substantial unrealized losses during that year. As such, it would have made sense for the fund to cut its distribution to preserve its net asset value, but this was clearly not the case. We should therefore take a look at this fund’s finances to determine how well it is covering its distribution. After all, no fund can pay out more than its investment returns over an extended period.

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities & Income Fund has maintained its track record for distribution consistency since the time of our last discussion. The fund has not changed the distribution at all over the period. However, it has released an updated financial report, which is the annual report for the full-year period that ended on Oct. 31, 2024. A link to this report was provided earlier in this article. As this is a newer report than the one that we had available to us previously, let us take a look at it for an update on the fund’s financial condition.

For the full-year period that ended on Oct. 31, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund received $81,292,027 in interest and $24,047,930 in net dividends from the assets in its portfolio. This gives the fund a total investment income of $105,339,957 for the full-year period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $49,808,887 available for shareholders. This was not sufficient to cover the $88,879,719 that the fund paid out in distributions over the period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up for the difference via capital gains. For the full-year period that ended on Oct. 31, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund reported net realized losses of $4,697,823, but these were more than offset by $196,346,064 in net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund’s net assets increased by $152,577,409 after accounting for all inflows and outflows during the period.

We can see that the fund managed to fully cover all of the distributions that it paid out, although it was only able to do so because of the substantial unrealized gains. This might be concerning given that unrealized gains can be erased by any correction in the market, and we have seen preferred stock prices decrease fairly significantly over the past several months. However, given the magnitude of the fund’s unrealized gains during the full-year period, I'm not particularly concerned about its ability to sustain its distribution going forward.

Valuation

Shares of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund are currently trading at a 4.63% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 4.82% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

However, the fund’s shares are currently trading at a somewhat expensive price compared to its long-term average. Over the past three years, shares of the Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 1.03% discount to a 13.97% discount on net asset value. The average price of the fund’s shares over the period has been a 7.58% discount on net asset value, which is quite a bit cheaper than the fund’s shares are priced at today.

Here's how this fund’s current valuation compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund

(4.63%)

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

(5.16%)

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Income Fund

(8.03%)

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

3.52%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

(1.64%)

We can see that the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund is currently trading at a premium on net asset value, while the rest of the funds are all trading at discounts. For the most part, the discounts are not very large, as we have seen in previous articles that some closed-end funds in other sectors boast double-digit discounts. These funds all have much smaller discounts. The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund is the median fund among this group in terms of value. As such, it does not appear to be especially expensive when compared to its peer group. However, when we consider that this fund has historically had a larger discount, it's difficult to say that it's especially attractively priced right now.

Overall, it might be better to try and get this fund at a price that is closer to its historical average. This would provide buyers with a larger margin of safety.

Expenses

The Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund has an expense ratio of 4.92% on net assets, which is a bit of an improvement over the 5.03% expense ratio that it had the last time that we discussed it. Despite being an improvement over the previous level, it obviously looks pretty expensive when compared to an index fund or something similar. In this case, the majority of the fund’s expenses come from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed to obtain leverage. If these expenses are excluded, the fund’s expense ratio comes in at a more reasonable 1.68%.

Here's how this fund compares with its peers:

Expense Ratio
